# **Task Documentation \- Programmer**

## Stars Align Video Game

# **Introduction**

Make sure you have downloaded the Ren’Py application from their [official website](https://www.renpy.org/latest.html), as in this document you will be taught how to build two of the puzzles in the Stars Align game.

# **The Game Engine Review**

To begin, create your project by **pressing the ‘Create New Project’ button**. **Name the project ‘Stars Align’** when prompted, and then **click on ‘Stars Align’ to select the project** on the left hand side as shown below.

![](task1.png)

You will be using a few specific buttons in the Ren’py game engine:

- **Navigate script**: this opens up a navigation window to all of the scripts your selected project has associated with it. Ren’Py automatically generates the following scripts: gui.rpy, options.rpy, screens.rpy, script.rpy.  
- Images: this will open up your images folder in your game’s folder in your file explorer. This is where you will be putting all your 2D game assets into.  
  - The path to this will look something like: Stars Align (or whatever name the project is) \-\> game \-\> images

![](task2.png)

This is what clicking on Navigate script will show you currently.  
![][image4]

If you **double click on script.rpy** it will open up the file in Visual Studio Code. Currently your code will look like this;  
label start:  
    scene bg room

    # This shows a character sprite. A placeholder is used, but you can  
    # replace it by adding a file named "eileen happy.png" to the images  
    # directory.  
    show eileen happy

    # These display lines of dialogue.  
    e "You've created a new Ren'Py game."

    e "Once you add a story, pictures, and music, you can release it to the world\!"

    # This ends the game.  
    return

The **label start indicates where the program begins to run from**. We will not be writing all of our puzzle code here, instead we will **create new labels and screens** that we will **call from the start label**.  
**Return as shown ends the game.**

If we **want to test the labels or screens** that we will be writing for the Stars Align project, we will be **using the start label as the place to debug our code by calling those methods**.

Now that you have created your first Ren’Py project, let’s add some puzzles to it\! The first puzzle you will be making is a grid puzzle. 

# **Puzzles**

Puzzles are screens within the game into which the player can enter and attempt to solve them.

## *Grid Puzzle*

The grid puzzle consists of a screen where a player can drag and drop various shapes onto a 6x6 grid. Their goal is to fit as many of the shapes into the grid as possible.

Create the Grid Screen:

1) Go into ‘Navigate Script’ and create a new script by pressing ‘+ Add script file’ and name it ‘grid.rpy’.

![](task3.png)

Now you should see this, a blank file for code.  
    # Ren'Py automatically loads all script files ending with .rpy. To use this  
    # file, define a label and jump to it from another file.

2) Create a screen called puzzle_board():

    screen puzzle_board():  
   

We create a screen and not a label because we want the items we will be placing into the scenery to interact with each other. If you create a label, it is much harder to program such interactions as they are designed more for dialogue and storyline for progression, not gameplay mechanics.

3) Create a blank image that matches the resolution of your computer screen and add it into the screen’s scene after placing it into your images folder in the game folder.

    screen puzzle_board():  
     
        # Background image  
        add "blank.png" xpos 0 ypos 0

4) Remove all the given code for you in the ‘label start’, except for the return statement.  
5) Now you will write into your start label your first debugging code. If you call the screen puzzle\_board() from it, once you launch the game and you press start once it loads in, you will see a blank screen with nothing to do in it, yet.

    label start:  
        call screen puzzle_board()

        # This ends the game.  
        return

Let’s add the actual grid now to the blank screen\!

Adding the Grid:

1) Above the ‘screen puzzle\_board()’ add ‘init python:’, this is where we will be defining our python variables and functions.

    init python:

2) We want to create a 6x6 grid in which each rectangle will be the same shape and size. Declare as shown the following variables to do just that.

    #Cell dimensions  
    cell_width = 195  
    cell_height = 138

    #Board dimensions  
    rows = 6  
    cols = 6

4) We now want to actually establish and create the grid itself. Use the following line of code to initialize an empty grid.

    #Create a 5x5 grid, initially empty (0 = empty, 1 = filled)  
    board = [[None for x in range(cols)] for y in range(rows)]

5)  Now we want to decide where on the board the actual board will be generated by Ren’Py. Establish the following starting values for x and y to make the grid appear slightly shifted to the left to allow for space for the draggable pieces of the puzzle.

    start_x = 100 #440   # top-left of grid (screen coords)  
    start_y = 100 #248

We have established the existence of the board but let’s now make Ren’Py actually place it into the scene.

In order to do this, first draw what you want your basic cell in the grid to look like. Mine will be as followed:  
![][image5]![][image6]  
The yellow is an empty cell, and the blue is a filled cell.

5) Going back to the screen add a ‘dragggroup:’ This is because we will want the player to eventually fill up the grid with various shapes. Thus, we must create the grid inside a draggroup so that the pieces and the grid’s cells can interact with each other.

screen puzzle_board():  
     
    # Background image  
    add "blank.png" xpos 0 ypos 0

    Draggroup:

6) Inside the draggroup now add the following code. This will actually generate the grid on your screen.

        # Loop through each cell in the board  
        for y in range(rows):  
            for x in range(cols):  
                drag:  
                    #tags the cell appropriately  
                    drag_name "cell_%d_%d" % (x, y)

                    #we want the cell to react to something getting dragged onto it, but not be droppable itself  
                    draggable False  
                    droppable True

                    #establishes location  
                    xpos 50 + x * cell_width  
                    ypos 50 + y * cell_height

                    #establishes size  
                    xysize (cell_width, cell_height)

                    #adds the images you created  
                    add ("emptyCell.png")

Using nested for loops we are traversing through the established ranges of 6 rows and 6 columns to establish each individual cell of the specified size at the calculated position.

You should now see the following on your screen if you launch the project and press start. Your grid will be completely yellow because nothing has been dragged and dropped into it yet.  
![][image7]  
Now let’s make it so we can drop shapes into these slots!

Create the Pieces:  
In order for the player to be able to drag and drop various shapes onto the grid, we must first declare the pieces themselves.

This is the first time we are declaring a list, although this one is a bit more complex as it is more like a list inside of a list. Inside this list we have the name of the shape, like “L” or “Square”, which is considered the key necessary to access the rest of the information associated with said shape, like its representative image as well as the shape defined in terms of (x,y) coordinates so that we can later use these mathematical values to calculate how it should be added to the grid.

1) Add this code into your ‘init python’ section of the code.

    #Pieces dictionary  
        pieces = {  
            "L": {  
                "shape": [(0,-1), (0,0), (0,1), (-1,1)],  # offsets  
                "image": "Lpuzzlepiece.png"  
            },  
            "Square": {  
                "shape": [(-1,-1), (0,-1), (-1,0), (0,0)],  
                "image": "Squarepuzzlepiece.png"  
            },

            "HorizontalT": {  
                "shape": [(0,0), (-1,-1), (0,-1), (1,-1)],  
                "image": "HorizontalT.png"  
            }  
        }

2) Make sure you create the images for your pieces. Here are mine for example:

![][image8]![][image9]![][image10]  
The coordinate locations are shown for the square so that you can figure out how you would design/format any original shapes yourself.

3) Add the following line below the code you just wrote:

    available_pieces = set(pieces.keys())  
This line will permit you to check which pieces are still available to be added to the grid, and which are located in the grid. We do this by setting initially all the keys (names of the mini lists, like “L” and “Square”), to be saved into ‘available\_pieces’.

4) We need to have an actual place for the pieces to be located. Create the following padding in the ‘init python’ block.

    sidebar_x = 1200  
    sidebar_y = 100  
    spacing = 20

5) Now let’s have Ren’Py actually place the blocks onto the screen. Inside the puzzle\_board() screen add the following for loop. Here we will traverse the list we created and add in each piece that is in the list as a ‘drag’, an item that a player can pick up and drop with their mouse.

    #Puzzle pieces in sidebar  
        for idx, (name, piece) in enumerate(pieces.items()):  
            if name in available_pieces:  
                drag:

6) Define the puzzle piece’s behaviour.

                    #tags the piece appropriately  
                    drag_name name

                    #clarify that the piece can be dragged and dropped onto another item, but that nothing will happen if things are dropped onto it  
                    draggable True  
                    droppable False

                    #Define the piece’s image and the piece’s physical location on the screen  
                    child piece["image"]

                    #Define the padding and formatting for the pieces so that it look good on the screen  
                    xpos sidebar_x  
                    ypos sidebar_y + idx * (cell_height + spacing)

This is what your screen should look like now. You may need to move the shapes around to see them all perfectly. The shapes currently can be dragged and dropped but will not be added to the grid.  
![][image11]

Create the Pieces Functions:

Now in order to actually do something with these pieces we will create three functions:
    - can_place()  
    - place_piece()  
    - remove_piece()

1) Add the following within your ‘init python’ block, since this is a function. This function will check if we can even place the specific shape into the particular location on the board. Into the method we are passing the board itself, the shape we are trying to drop into the board and x and y positions of the cell you are trying to drop the shape into.

#FUNCTIONS

    # Check if piece fits at a position  
    def can_place(board, shape, x, y):

2) Traverse the x and y coordinates of the dropped shape and calculate the location the shape would occupy based on the figure’s shape. Waning, the shape cannot be rotated\! The user can only drop it as is into the grid.

        #dx and dy are just our xs and ys that we labeled within our shapes to define their structures in the mini coordinate mapping  
        for dx, dy in shape:  
            #based off where we are actually trying to drop the shape we calculate the practical location of the shape on the grid  
            nx = x + dx  
            ny = y + dy

3) Check if the calculated grid position is out of bounds for the grid. If it is out of bounds, return False, that we cannot place the shape.

            # Out of bounds  
            if nx < 0 or nx >= cols or ny < 0 or ny >= rows:  
                return False

4) Check if the cell the player is trying to drop into is already filled, then return False because we cannot drop into a filled cell.

            # Cell already filled  
            if board[ny][nx] is not None:  
                return False  
        return True

5) Now let’s create a function that will let us place a piece onto the grid. Add the following within your ‘init python’ block, since this is a function. Into this function we pass the board, shape, x, y, like in the previous function, and the piece\_name which is the tag that indicates what shape we are trying to drop into the grid.

#Place piece on the board  
    def place_piece(board, shape, x, y, piece_name):

6) Traverse the list like we did in the previous function. Recalculate the position that the cell should be occupying and set those cells (the defined coordinates of the shape, recalculated to be on the actual grid) to be occupied by the piece.

        for dx, dy in shape:  
            nx = x + dx  
            ny = y + dy  
            board[ny][nx] = piece_name

7) Create the function to remove the piece from the grid. If called this function traverses through all the rows and columns of the board, and checks each cell if it is occupies by the passed in piece_name.

    def remove_piece(board, piece_name):  
        for y in range(rows):  
            for x in range(cols):

8)  Set the cell that had been occupied to be empty. 

                if board\[y\]\[x\] \== piece\_name:  
                    board\[y\]\[x\] \= None

9)  Add the now available shape to the available list of shapes that the user can try and add to the grid.

        # Return the piece back to available pieces  
        available_pieces.add(piece_name)

10) With the current code if we were to fail dropping the piece onto the grid, it would remain on the grid. Let’s have it jump back to the padded area we created for all the pieces by adding the following code to the ‘init python’ block.

#Home positions for pieces (where they go back if placement fails)  
    piece_home = {}  
    i = 0  
    for name in pieces.keys():  
        piece_home[name] = (sidebar_x, sidebar_y + i * (cell_height + spacing))  
        i += 1

Currently the code we wrote doesn’t actually do anything because none of the functions are called. Let’s now write and call a method that will make the player able to actually drag, drop and remove pieces.

Callback Method:

1) Declare the following method in the ‘init python’ block. The first x and y values we pass in are of the cell we are trying to drop into and the second round of values are necessary tags for the item being dragged and the target into which it is being dropped. We include a basic error handler that makes sure the dragged and dropped item is in fact a draggable/droppable item. 

    #Create a callback for each cell  
    def make_drop_callback(x, y):  
        def callback(drop_target, drags):  
            if not drags:  
                return None

2) Establish what the dragged object is and its name.

            dragged = drags[0]  
            drag_name = dragged.drag_name

3) Include this code in your program. This is a good way to debug and check if the shape you are dropping into the grid is actually operating correctly. It also gives feedback about where the shape is being dropped into on the grid. You will remove this in your final product since the user doesn’t need to see this information. Renpy.log and renpy.notify are built in, default functions.

            renpy.log("Dropped %r onto cell (%d,%d)" % (drag_name, y, x))  
            renpy.notify("Dropped %s on %d,%d" % (drag_name, y, x))

4) Call the can_place() function you declared to check if a piece can be placed. If it can be placed (returns True), call place\_piece() to add the piece down.

            if can_place(board, pieces[drag_name]["shape"], x, y):  
                place_piece(board, pieces[drag_name]["shape"], x, y, drag_name)

5) Remove the piece you just added from the available_pieces list. 

                # Remove piece from available pieces so it disappears  
                if drag_name in available_pieces:  
                    available_pieces.remove(drag_name)

6) If can\_place() returns False, and you cannot place down the piece, write the following code to snap the piece back to the original location that it.

            else:  
                hx, hy = piece_home.get(drag_name, (sidebar_x, sidebar_y))  
                try:  
                    dragged.snap(hx, hy, delay=0.15)  
                except Exception:  
                    pass

7) Add this notify statement to tell you if a piece cannot be dropped at a particular spot.

                renpy.notify("Can't place %s at %d,%d" % (drag_name, y, x))  
                #return False

8) We include this return statement to exit out of the method.

        return callback

We have written this callback method but the program still doesn’t actually let you drop the pieces into the grid Why? This is because we haven’t called the callback method (and thereby subsequent methods) from the screen. 

9) Add the following code to the draggroup at the bottom, inside the block. 

    dropped make_drop_callback(x, y)

Now that we have created a filled and empty cell, as well as a way for a cell to be empty vs filled, change the code inside the following block: for idx, (name, piece) in enumerate(pieces.items()):   
Change:  
    add ("emptyCell.png")  
To this:  
    #adds the images you created  
    add ("filledCell.png" if board[y]\[x] else "emptyCell.png")

This is what the screen should look like now if the player drags and drops a shape into the grid.  
![][image12]

Now the player can drag and drop shapes onto the grid, however they cannot remove the shape from the screen.

10) The last change is to add the following line of code into the draggroup block at the very bottom. This code allows the player to click on the shape they just dropped and remove it out of the grid. Now the user can place it into a different part of the grid if they want.

clicked If(board[y][x] is not None, Function(remove_piece, board, board[y][x]))

Potential Uses for the Grid Puzzle:  
The original goal is for the player to fill up the whole space with shapes that they will unlock as they progress through the story of ‘Stars Align’, however as the game is still in development and incomplete, and especially if you are using this as a reference for another game you are building yourself here is a couple different ways you can use this puzzle:

- Fill in with all the shapes.  
- Fill in with only specific shapes that a player can collect.  
- The player has to create a specific image inside the grid out of the little pieces.  
- The player has to use the blocks to make a pathway going from one end of the grid to the other.

## *Book  Puzzle*

Book Puzzle is a puzzle in which a player will be able to click on books to switch their order, to put them into the correct order. 

The Setup:

1) Create a new script, like before, and name it ‘bookPuzzle.rpy’.  
2) Remove any auto generated code and create a new screen.

screen bookPuzzle():

3) Create a bookshelf asset on your own and add it to the screen.

screen bookPuzzle():

    add "bg_bookshelf.png" xpos 0 ypos 0

4) Go back to your ‘script.rpy’, comment out the previous call statement you had and add the call screen to the screen you just created.

#The game starts here.  
label start:

    call screen bookPuzzle()  
    #call screen puzzle_board()

    # This ends the game.  
    return

Here is an example of how my screen looks like (white background with a brown bookshelf) when the screen is launched.  
![][image13]

5) Design your three books, and what they look like if they are selected (clicked on by the user). This is what the books I will be using look like for example. The darker colored counterpart is the selected version of the book.

![][image14]![][image15]![][image16]![][image17]![][image18]![][image19]

6) Now we need to add these images into the program as variables in lists that we can access and display them. Create a list of the regular book images. Make sure to put the following steps into an ‘init python’ block.

    book_images = {  
        "A": "bookA.png",  
        "B": "bookB.png",  
        "C": "bookC.png",  
    }

7) Create a list of the selected book images.

    book_selected_images = {  
        "A": "bookA_selected.png",  
        "B": "bookB_selected.png",  
        "C": "bookC_selected.png",  
    }

8) Declare what the order of books will be when the player turns on the game.

    current_books = ["C", "B", "A"]

9)  Declare what the order of books should be.

    correct_books = ["A", "B", "C"]

10)  We need to keep track of which book the player clicks on first so that we can do the swap. Declare the following variable and set it initially to none since in the beginning of the game the player hasn’t selected any book yet.

first_choice = None

Now let us create a function that allows the books to be swapped.

Declaring The Swap Function:

1) Declare in the ‘init python:’ block the swap_books() function. Pass into it an ‘index’ value. This index value is going to be passed in later from the screen, with it being either equal to the first book that the player clicks on, or the second one.

def swap_books(index):

2) Declare first_choice, current_books, and correct_books variables as global to be able to access and change them from inside this function.

        global first_choice, current_books, correct_books

3) Write the following statement to set the first_choice variable equal to the passed in index, if the first_choice variable has not been given a value yet.

        if first_choice is None:  
            first_choice = index

4) Write the following code. If the first_choice already has an index value, then the index being passed in is the second book with which we want to swap. This line of code will actually perform the swap between the two books.

        else:  
            current_books[first_choice], current_books[index] = current_books[index], current_books[first_choice]

5) Reset the first_choice back to none so that the player can select books to swap again.

            first_choice = None

6) Write the following code to check if the current arrangement of books matches the correct order books. If yes, the player will be notified (using the built in notify system in Ren’Py that they solved it).

            if current_books == correct_books:  
                renpy.notify("You solved it!")

Now that we have set everything up and created our function, let’s put it into action in the actual screen.

Adding Everything to the Screen:

1) Declare a fixed block in the bookPuzzle() screen. 

We use ‘fixed’ because unlike ‘hbox’ or ‘vbox’, this block allows us to dictate the exact positions of each included element using position properties like xpos, ypos, align, etc, relative to the top-left corner of the ‘fixed’ container itself. Furthermore, it allows us to include overlapping elements, while in ‘hbox’ or ‘vbox’ elements would be stacked neatly.  
fixed:

2) Declare the starting positions of the first book as well as the shelf so that the books look like they are sitting on the shelf.

        $ xpos_row = 50  # starting X position of the first book  
        $ y_shelf = 978  # Y coordinate of the top of the shelf (so books sit on it)

3) Declare a for loop which will go through the current_books list and display the books in the order we had written them to be. 

        for i, book in enumerate(current_books):        

4) Get the size (width and height) of the displayed book (the specific i in the list), so that when the swap method is called the two books are swapped out neatly and no weird blank space is left behind, nor do they overlap.

            # Get image size  
            $ w, h = renpy.image_size(book_images[book])

5) If the book the player has clicked on is the one that the for loop is currently on (as it is traversing), make it be displayed as selected (using the book art we made and declared earlier).

            if first_choice == i:  
                # If this book is currently selected  
                $ idle_img = book_selected_images[book]  
                $ hover_img = book_selected_image[book]

6) If this book has not been clicked, make it be displayed as not selected.

            else:  
                $ idle_img = book_images[book]  
                $ hover_img = book_images[book]

7) Declare the book to be an imagebutton so that we can call the swap\_books() function, if the book is clicked.

            # Bottom-align the book  
            imagebutton:

8) Apply image assets so that it loops appropriately.

                idle idle_img  
                hover hover_img

9) Declare positions based on previously calculated lengths and widths so that everything is displayed correctly.

                xpos xpos_row  
                ypos y_shelf - h

10) Call the swap_books() function, passing into it the index of either the first or second book clicked, which will either mark said clicked on book as selected or complete the swap with the second book the player has clicked on.

                action Function(swap_books, i)

11) Add this last line of code outside the imagebutton block but inside the for loop, to make sure all of the books displayed as a group are in the proper position and aligned with the bookshelf.

            $ xpos_row += w

Now that you have added all these lines of code you should have a functioning book puzzle! Your screen should look like this.  
![][image20]  
This is what it looks like when I select A, and swap it with C. A is clearly selected in this image

![][image21]  
A and C have been swapped and the player has been notified that the books have been put into the proper order (alphabetical).

![][image22]
